title: [nlir][onboarding] checkout refresh persistently fails on ms-mac — git fetch 'No user exists for uid 501', beads stale
description:
## Symptom (routed by log-monitor-msm -> ctrl, 2026-07-03)

nlir (newly onboarded ~13:53 today) has a PERSISTENTLY failing checkout refresh on ms-mac: `POST /api/v1/projects/nlir/checkout/refresh -> 500` (fatal=false) recurring every sweep, keeping nlir's beads STALE (board shows nlir as one of the stale projects).

## Root cause (from ms-mac daemon.log)

    checkout refresh error for nlir (consecutive failures: 40): daemon error: git fetch origin main failed: No user exists for uid 501

40+ consecutive failures. The underlying `git fetch origin main` for nlir's checkout fails with "No user exists for uid 501" (getpwuid(501) failing — uid 501 = the macOS operator user).

## Key discriminator

This is nlir-SPECIFIC: cacophony's checkout refresh on the SAME ms-mac daemon (pid 81113, healthy, ~6h uptime) works fine. So it is NOT a general daemon-env failure — something about nlir's checkout/remote differs.

## NOT the bd-95abff class

This is a git-fetch-ENV error (uid resolution during git fetch), not the bd-95abff silent-materialization-wedge class. Different root; do not fold.

## Fix directions

1. Compare nlir's remote/checkout config vs cacophony's — likely nlir uses a remote type (e.g. SSH) or a git config (core.sshCommand / a credential helper) that triggers a getpwuid(501) lookup the daemon's launchd git-subprocess env can't satisfy, whereas cacophony's remote doesn't.
2. If it's a daemon git-subprocess env gap (missing HOME/USER for the fetch), `services.caco-daemon-extra-env` is inherited by the daemon's child git/subprocess operations (per AGENTS.md) so HOME/USER could be set there — but confirm WHY cacophony is unaffected first before a blanket env change.
3. This is a NEW-PROJECT ONBOARDING settling issue — the nlir owner / config-onboarder (Harry onboarded it ~13:53) should verify nlir's remote + checkout config.

Non-fatal, nlir-specific, ms-mac daemon otherwise healthy. Routed by log-monitor-msm; ctrl found the root cause + filed. cc Harry (nlir onboarder).

## Diagnostic (msm-0, read-only, 2026-07-03 ~19:40)

Refutes fix-direction #1 and narrows to a daemon env bug:

- **Both nlir AND cacophony use `ssh://git@github.com/...` remotes** (checked `caco config show --section projects`). Not a remote-type difference.
- **Both daemon canonical checkouts (`~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/{nlir,cacophony}`) have IDENTICAL local git config**: same `core.sshCommand = ssh -F /dev/null -i ~/.ssh/caco -o IdentitiesOnly=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new`, same `remote.origin.url` class, and BOTH have local `user.name`/`user.email` set. So the failure is NOT a checkout-config difference.
- **nlir's checkout is proven functional**: `git -C ~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/nlir ls-remote origin main` (HOME set, normal env) returns the ref cleanly. The key/remote/config all work.
- The `No user exists for uid 501` is git's `getpwuid(501)` failing to resolve HOME when HOME is unset in the fetch subprocess. Since cacophony succeeds on the SAME daemon with IDENTICAL config, this is a **daemon env-propagation bug**: the git-fetch subprocess for the newly-onboarded nlir project is missing HOME (existing projects like cacophony inherit it; a project onboarded mid-daemon-run at ~13:53 apparently does not).

### Recommended fix (daemon/operator lane — NOT nlir checkout)
1. Simplest: **restart the ms-mac caco-daemon** so nlir's fetch subprocess inherits HOME like cacophony's (likely clears the 40-consec-failure state, since onboarding mid-run didn't propagate env).
2. If it persists after restart: set `HOME=/Users/harryaskham` (and `USER`) in `services.caco-daemon-extra-env` so every daemon git subprocess has it, or fix the onboarding path to propagate the daemon's env to per-project fetch subprocesses.

nlir's checkout/remote/config need no change. Handing to Harry/ctrl (daemon owners).
creator: ms-mac:cacophony:ms-mac-cacophony-caco-ctrl

title: llm-mode operand coercion doesn't use the LLM coercion fallback (eval uses deterministic-only Value::coerce)
description:
Found dogfooding nlir in LLM mode (Harry's directive). In `--mode llm`, operand coercion does NOT use the LLM coercion fallback, so a non-numeric operand to a number operator errors instead of being interpreted by the model.

Repro: `nlir --mode llm -e "'ten'+'twenty'"` -> "cannot coerce string `ten` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number" (exit 1). SPEC explicitly wants this to work: "an LLM coercion — interpret the text as a value of type T ... turns a vague 'ten to twenty' into 15." Expected: 'ten'->10, 'twenty'->20, add -> 30.

Root cause: src/eval.rs coerces operands via `Value::coerce(target, sep)` (the deterministic-only path, bd-20df97) at all 4 coercion sites (lines ~257, 344, 560, 614), never `llm::coerce_with_llm` (the deterministic->LLM->error fallback, bd-ecb930). So step-3 LLM coercion from the config `types:` map is never invoked in llm mode. The misleading error text ("or model coercion") comes from Value::coerce's CoerceError::Unrepresentable, which describes the model step even though Value::coerce never runs it.

This is the eval<->coercion seam (mirrors the realise_llm seam bd-dc3c72): aur-2 owns the coercion API (coerce_with_llm), aur-1 owns eval.rs. Proposed fix: aur-2 adds `llm::coerce_operand(value, target, &Config, sep, mode, env_lookup, cli_model) -> Result<Value, LlmCoerceError>` that dispatches det -> Value::coerce, llm -> coerce_with_llm; eval.rs adds EvalError::LlmCoerce + From<LlmCoerceError> and swaps the 4 `.coerce(...)` sites to it (mode-aware). Det mode behaviour unchanged.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Decide where MessageViews SPEC defaults live (config resolve vs messages fallback)
description: SPEC §Example config defines canonical `context.messages.views` defaults (`^`=[assistant], `^_`=[user], `^*`=[user,assistant,system], `^/`=[system]), but config `MessageViews::default()` is all-empty and `resolve_defaults` (src/config.rs) does not populate them. title: Parallelism may not concurrency-run independent llm operand subcalls (30s for 4-way; investigate scheduler vs HTTP-client mutex vs endpoint)
description:
Driving nlir in llm mode: independent LLM operand subtrees do NOT appear to run concurrently despite the parallelism epic (bd-a32894, std::thread::scope, defaults.parallelism=8). Evidence (anthropic_messages HTTP backend against a real endpoint):
- single `!('the sky is blue')` ≈ 4s
- `!(a)&!(b)` (2 independent ! + &) ≈ 14s
- `!(a)&!(b)&!(c)&!(d)` (4 independent ! + &) ≈ 30s

30s ≈ 5 sequential ~5s calls; concurrent would be ~2 call-times (~10s). So the 4 independent `!` subcalls are running sequentially, not concurrently.

Needs investigation (aur-1's parallelism lane): does the scheduler parallelize the operands of a single n-ary Apply (the `&` operands)? Is the anthropic_messages ureq HTTP client behind a shared mutex that serializes? Or does the endpoint rate-limit? Reproduce with a command-backend model (claude CLI) to rule out endpoint serialization. Config: defaults.parallelism=8, per-operand `!` uses model sonnet (anthropic_messages). Found by msm-0 while building the llm-mode working-cases library; may be endpoint-side, hence P3 pending confirmation.

## Refined diagnosis (msm-0 + aur-1, 2026-07-03)

NOT a scheduler bug. Confirmed the scheduler + COMMAND backends DO parallelize:
- A `command:` operator that `sleep 2`s: 4-way `~a&~b&~c&~d` = 2.1s (not 8s) — concurrent.
- aur-1: 3 concurrent subject extractions via command (claude/pi CLI) joined ~13s — parallelism live.

So the ~30s serialization I saw is SPECIFIC to the `anthropic_messages` HTTP path against the LiteLLM endpoint (helsinki:4000). nlir uses a FRESH `ureq::post` per call (no shared client/Mutex in llm.rs), so it is NOT a nlir lock. Most likely LiteLLM/Claude endpoint concurrency limits serialize the concurrent HTTP requests, or ureq's default agent behavior. Downgrading: probably endpoint-side, not a nlir bug. To fully confirm nlir fires concurrent HTTP, a local sleeping mock-HTTP anthropic server would settle it. Separately noticed `--parallelism 1` did not force sequential in the sleep test (flag→scheduler wiring gap worth a small check).

## RESOLVED — direct HTTP path DOES parallelize (aur-1, real-endpoint evidence)

Tested the anthropic_messages HTTP backend against the LiteLLM endpoint at the CORRECT URL: http://helsinki.miku-owl.ts.net:4000/v1 (note: the operator's given URL http://.../v1 was missing the :4000 port → port 80 connection-refused from aurora; LITELLM_BASE_URL=http://helsinki.miku-owl.ts.net:4000). Config defaults.model=direct, op with no model override (so the direct backend actually runs, not a command backend).

Clean before/after timing (3 concurrent subject extractions + join):
- single #'x' baseline = 4s
- #'a'&#'b'&#'c' at parallelism 8 = 8s (~2x baseline = 3 subjects CONCURRENT + 1 join)
- same at --parallelism 1 = 17s (~4x baseline = fully sequential)

8s vs 17s => the direct HTTP path parallelizes correctly. The scheduler parallelizes ALL backends (command AND anthropic_messages HTTP). The earlier ~30s 4-way observation was transient endpoint behaviour (cold/loaded), NOT a systematic nlir serialization or client-mutex bug. No mock-HTTP server needed — real-endpoint data settles it. Closing.

(The --parallelism-flag-not-wired sub-note was split to bd-e9dedc and is LANDED.)
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Concurrent in-flight subcall dedup (two simultaneous identical LLM calls both compute)
description: The per-run realisation cache (bd-1d078c) is a Mutex<HashMap> that dedups SEQUENTIAL identical subcalls, but two truly-simultaneous identical subcalls on different scoped threads (bd-780dbf) may BOTH miss the cache before either stores, so both compute — no in-flight dedup. For pure det ops this is harmless (same result); for identical concurrent LLM calls it means a duplicate model call. Fix: an in-flight registry keyed by the realise cache key holding a shared "computing" placeholder (Condvar/OnceLock) so a second thread waits for the first's result. Low priority; correctness holds either way. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-780dbf/bd-1d078c parallelism session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Wire double-quote $name interpolation into eval (lexer must carry the quote kind)
description:
SPEC §Interpolation: a double-quoted string `"…$name…"` interpolates bare `$name` at eval time; single-quoted `'…'` (raw) and bare literals do not. The interpolation FUNCTION exists (context::interpolate, bd-22fa7e), but it is NOT wired into the evaluator, so `nlir -e 'k=x;"v is $k"'` yields the literal "v is $k" instead of "v is x".

Root cause: the lexer collapses both `"…"` and `'…'` into a single `Token::Quoted(String)` with no flag distinguishing interpolating (double) from raw (single) quotes, so the parser's `Expr::Quoted` can't tell eval whether to interpolate.

Fix (cross-lane):
1. Lexer (msm-0's lane): carry the quote kind — e.g. `Token::Quoted { content, interpolate: bool }` (true for `"…"`, false for `'…'`) or a distinct token — and likewise `Expr::Quoted`/a new `Expr::Interpolated` in the parser.
2. Eval (aur-1): in eval, call `self.context.interpolate(&content)` for interpolating strings (I already have Context::interpolate); leave raw/bare literal.

Discovered by aur-1 while adding greedy eval-time read tests (bd-91e573). I can do the eval-side wiring once the lexer/parser carries the interpolate flag.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: config.example.yaml command models: YAML folded-scalar keeps newlines, breaking bash command backends
description:
Found while dogfooding nlir in LLM mode (Harry's directive, 2026-07-03). The shipped config.example.yaml `command:` model fields use a YAML `>` folded scalar with MORE-INDENTED continuation lines, e.g.:

  command: >
    claude --model claude-sonnet-5
      --system-prompt "..."
      --print "..."

Per the YAML folded-scalar spec, lines indented more than the block's first content line are kept as LITERAL newlines (the "more-indented" exception) rather than folded to spaces. So the resulting command string has real newlines, and `bash -c` runs each line as a SEPARATE statement: `claude --model claude-sonnet-5` (no prompt), then `--print "..."` -> "bash: --print: command not found" (exit 127). Any command-model realisation from the shipped example is broken.

Repro: nlir --mode llm -e "#'the quick brown fox'" with a claude command model authored via `>` folded + indented continuations -> "command backend exited with status 127: ... --print: command not found".

FIX: author command: fields as SINGLE-LINE commands (or a `|` literal block that is valid multi-line bash). Note the echo operator's `command: |` literal block is CORRECT (intentional multi-line bash); the bug is specifically the `>` folded style with unintended-newline continuations. Also update the models to Harry's requested real backends: claude (claude-sonnet-5 via the claude binary) + copilot (github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6 via pi; copilot has no claude-sonnet-5 on the Responses API). Keep example_config_parses_and_validates green.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Dedup negative-index-from-end resolver (stack + messages resolve_index)
description: `Stack::resolve_index` (src/stack.rs) and `messages::resolve_index` (src/messages.rs) are the identical negative-index-from-end resolver: `index >= 0` from the start, `index < 0` from the end (`-1` = last), `None` out of range. Both back SPEC index semantics (`$N` stack index with `$0` bottom / `$-1` top; `^N` message index). A shared helper (e.g. `fn resolve_index(len, i64) -> Option<usize>` in a small `index`/util module, or one made pub and reused) would dedup ~6 lines and keep the semantics in one place. Low priority; the two are currently in-sync. Filed via reflect-session mixin from bd-d4631b/bd-22fa7e session.

title: Division renders full f64 precision (10/3 -> 3.3333333333333335) — decide display precision
description:
Found dogfooding. Non-terminating division renders at full f64 precision, which is ugly for an English-output IR: `nlir --mode det -e '10/3'` -> "3.3333333333333335". Clean fractions are fine (1/2 -> 0.5, 7/2 -> 3.5, 100/8 -> 12.5); only repeating decimals surface the raw f64 shortest-round-trip string.

Location: Value::format_number in src/value.rs (aur-2 lane). This is a design decision, not a clear bug — options: (a) leave as-is (exact, deterministic, round-trippable); (b) round non-integral results to N significant figures for display (loses precision, changes value semantics if the rounded value feeds further ops); (c) only round at final stdout render, keeping full precision internally. SPEC doesn't specify float display precision and has no division examples. Filing as a draft for team/operator input before changing value semantics. Related: bd-f551f9 (bare float literals like 3.14 don't lex yet — msm-0 lexer lane); a display-precision decision should be consistent with literal float support.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Math precedence broken by default: default_priority ignores the SPEC ladder for arithmetic sigils
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. parser::default_priority only keys off FIXITY (prefix 14, postfix 1, infix/mixfix = DEFAULT_PRIORITY 9). So a config that declares the canonical arithmetic operators WITHOUT explicit `priority:` gets +, *, ** all at priority 9 → `1+2*3` evaluates to 9, not 7. The SPEC §Grammar precedence ladder (** 13, * / 12, + - 11, & | 9) is documented as the intended precedence but is NOT applied by default, and the SPEC §Example config `operators:` block omits explicit priorities on add/mul/sub/div/pow — so a config author copying the SPEC example gets mathematically-wrong precedence.

Verified: with explicit `priority: 11/12/13` on +-/*//** the parser respects them correctly (1+2*3=7, 2**3*2=16, 2+3*4-1=13). So the parser is fine; the gap is the DEFAULTS + the SPEC example.

Options: (a) update the SPEC §Example config to include the precedence ladder priorities on the arithmetic ops (minimal); (b) add a sigil-aware default-priority resolution for the canonical arithmetic sigils (**,*,/,+,-) so the SPEC ladder holds without explicit config (more magic, less config-driven); (c) document loudly that arithmetic operators MUST set priority. Filed by aur-1 from the nlir LLM-mode driving session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

description:

title: llm-mode coercion bypasses type rules: list->number is LLM-coerced (invents a number) instead of a hard error
description:
Found dogfooding nlir in LLM mode. `nlir --mode llm -e "[1,2]+3"` returns 6 instead of a loud error. Per SPEC (Types & coercion) and the landed coercion layer (bd-20df97), `list -> number` is ALWAYS an error and must never be routed to the LLM. But eval's coerce_operand (src/eval.rs, from bd-ba9f85) handles the llm branch by calling llm::realise_llm directly with the types.number model+prompt, which asks the model to interpret the rendered list ("1\n2") as a number -> it invents one (here 3), so [1,2]+3 -> 6. This violates the type-system hard rule and silently produces a wrong result.

Root cause: coerce_operand reimplements the coercion flow (deterministic -> realise_llm) instead of delegating to the types-layer coercion API (llm::coerce_with_llm, bd-ecb930), which enforces the list->number hard error and the deterministic/loud-error rules. realise_llm is the OPERATOR-realisation seam, not the coercion seam.

Fix options: (a) make coerce_operand call llm::coerce_with_llm in llm mode (it already does deterministic-first, list->number hard error, model coercion via the types.model/prompt, and UnparseableResult on a non-T answer) instead of realise_llm; or (b) add an explicit list->number (and other never-LLM) guard in coerce_operand before the realise_llm call. Option (a) also fixes: caching (coerce_with_llm can front the CoercionCache bd-876367 -- coercions are currently NOT cached), and correct loud errors when the model returns a non-number. eval.rs is msm-0/aur-1 lane; this is the types-coercion semantics (aur-2 lane) -- coordinate the wire like the realise_llm seam.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Nested parallelization (parallelize deeply-nested independent subcalls, not just top-level)
description: The parallel DAG scheduler (bd-780dbf) parallelizes only the TOP-LEVEL operands of each Apply: a parallelized operand subtree evaluates SEQUENTIALLY inside (eval_parallel_safe does not spawn nested threads). This bounds total concurrency simply (max ~parallelism per Apply) but does not run deeply-nested independent LLM/command calls concurrently — e.g. in `(#a&#b) & (#c&#d)` the inner pairs run sequentially within their outer-operand thread. A nested scheduler (a shared bounded semaphore/pool across recursion, or a work-stealing queue) would lift this while keeping the global parallelism bound. Also: once nested parallelization exists, the backtick Serial handling (bd-f66c32) becomes load-bearing (it must prevent nested parallelization inside a `\`` subtree) — currently vacuous since nothing nests. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-780dbf parallelism session.

title: config: env-interp clobbers bare $vars in command: bash blocks
description:
Config env-interpolation (bd-b342fd) expands a BARE `$name` inside `command:` bash blocks, clobbering shell variables that collide with a SET env var. Concretely: aur-1's `@`/echo command uses bash `$out`; the nix dev-shell exports `out` (nix build output path), so config interp rewrites `$out` -> that path and the command breaks. Passes in clean CI where `out` is unset, so it is an environment-dependent flake (surfaced via eval::tests::command_realisation_runs_under_bash).

Options to consider (SPEC decision needed): (a) config interp only expands `${VAR}` braces, not bare `$name` (but SPEC line ~330 uses bare `$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`); (b) skip interpolation entirely for `command:` values and let bash do its own expansion; (c) only expand names that are explicitly config-referenced, leaving unknown `$name` literal; (d) require `$$` escaping in command blocks. Recommend (b): do not env-interpolate `command:` blocks. Owner: config lane (msm-0). Low urgency (env-specific) but a real footgun for anyone whose shell exports a colliding var.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: [build] Linux x86 nix dev-shell coreutils segfaulting
description:
Reported by Harry (2026-07-03): on Linux x86, coreutils tools segfault inside the nlir `nix develop` dev shell, blocking `nix develop --command cargo build` for the Aurora agents.

Context / likely cause:
- nlir's flake pins nixpkgs to collective's exact rev (flakehub tarball rev 0590cd39) per operator request, to reuse the cached toolchain.
- The dev shell is only required on macOS to supply `libiconv` for the `-liconv` link (ureq/rustls TLS stack via feedback-cli/updatable-cli). On Linux, glibc already provides iconv.

WORKAROUND for Linux x86 (unblocks Aurora agents now):
- Build WITHOUT the dev shell: plain `cargo build` with a system Rust toolchain should link fine on Linux (no `-liconv` needed). Then `cargo fmt`/`clippy`/`test` directly.
- If a system Rust toolchain is unavailable, the nix rustc/cargo binaries themselves are fine; only coreutils in the interactive shell env is segfaulting — invoke tools by absolute path or use `nix build .#nlir` for a one-shot binary.

Diagnostics needed (from a Linux x86 node):
1. Is this nlir-specific or do OTHER cacophony project dev shells (omni-cli, tendril, collective) also segfault coreutils on the same node? If all projects, it is environmental (node/store), not the nlir flake.
2. Which exact coreutils tool + command segfaults (e.g. `cp`, `mkdir`, `cat`)? `dmesg`/exit signal?
3. Does `nix store verify --repair /nix/store/...coreutils...` or re-fetch fix it (partial/corrupt download)?
4. Does omni-cli (which pins github nixos-unstable, a NEWER nixpkgs) work on the same node? If yes, the pinned rev 0590cd39's coreutils is the culprit on that hardware and we may need a devShell nixpkgs bump for Linux.

Filed from the nlir config-vertical session (msm-0). Not claimed: needs a Linux x86 repro to fix.

MITIGATION LANDED (Harry, c08b7a9): removed `use flake` from `.envrc` so direnv no longer auto-loads the nix dev shell on `cd`. Practical build workflow now:
- Linux x86: build with the SYSTEM Rust toolchain — plain `cargo build` / `cargo test` / `cargo clippy` / `cargo fmt`. No dev shell needed (glibc provides iconv), which sidesteps the segfaulting dev-shell coreutils.
- macOS: still needs `libiconv`, so build via an explicit `nix develop --command cargo build` (the dev shell is fine on macOS; only Linux x86 coreutils segfaults).
Bead stays OPEN as a tracked known-issue: the underlying reason the pinned-rev dev-shell coreutils segfaults on Linux x86 is still undiagnosed and could resurface for `nix build`/CI. Diagnostics in the original description still wanted.

DIAGNOSTICS (aurora Linux x86_64, NixOS 6.18.32, nix 2.34.7; 2026-07-03, aur-2):

NOT REPRODUCIBLE on aurora now. Findings against the original questions:

1. nlir-specific vs environmental: NEITHER segfaults currently. nlir dev shell
   coreutils-9.8 (/nix/store/wkkwxc04gdw6b263l1h29pjarjnjdyb6-coreutils-9.8) AND
   omni-cli dev shell coreutils-9.11
   (/nix/store/9ypz3flqsrl5xl495mm8h645gadjsxi1-coreutils-9.11) both run
   cp/mkdir/cat/ls/mv/rm with exit=0.
2. Which tool segfaults: NONE currently -- cp/mkdir/cat/ls/mv/rm all exit=0 in
   the nlir dev shell.
3. store verify/repair: "nix store verify --no-trust <coreutils-9.8 path>"
   returns clean (exit 0) -- the path is intact, NOT corrupt now.
4. omni-cli (newer nixpkgs): works (coreutils-9.11). AND nlir's own pinned-rev
   coreutils-9.8 works too.

Also verified the original failing command path: "nix develop path:<nlir>
--command" runs cargo 1.91.0 + rustc 1.91.1 fine (TOOLCHAIN-OK).

CONCLUSION: The original segfault was almost certainly TRANSIENT store corruption
(a partial/corrupt download of the coreutils-9.8 store path) at report time,
since re-fetched/repaired -- the coreutils-9.8 path now verifies clean and works.
The pinned rev 0590cd39 is EXONERATED (a clean coreutils-9.8 works; no devShell
nixpkgs bump needed). The system-toolchain mitigation (c08b7a9) is the durable
Linux workflow and CI is green on it. Recommend resolving; reopen if the segfault
recurs (transient corruption can recur on a fresh partial download on any node).
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: LLM-mode type coercion unimplemented: eval coerces operands deterministically only (types.model/prompt unused)
description:
Driving nlir in llm mode against a real Claude backend surfaced that LLM-mode type coercion is UNIMPLEMENTED. `nlir -e "'five' + 'three'"` (llm mode; `+` has operands: number) fails with `cannot coerce string \`five\` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number`.

Root cause: `Evaluator::eval_apply` (src/eval.rs ~341-345 and the concurrent path ~612-614) coerces operands with `value.coerce(op_cfg.operands, &sep)` — DETERMINISTIC-only. The SPEC §Types llm fallback (config `types: number: { model, prompt, schema }`) is never invoked, so a non-numeric string can never be coerced via the model even in llm mode.

Fix: add an evaluator coercion that, when deterministic coercion fails AND mode==Llm AND config.types has the target type's model+prompt, calls the model (llm::realise_llm with the type's prompt over the rendered value) and parses the answer to the target type. String target still stringifies deterministically. Found by msm-0 while building the llm-mode working-cases library.

title: M^N message-range is unreachable: range logic exists but no AST node / parser production / eval arm
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. The SPEC documents message ranges `M^N` (e.g. `(1+1)^(5+5)` = assistant messages 2..10, joined with _sep; worked-examples table row `#0^3;#^-1;&`). The RANGE LOGIC exists — messages.rs::MessageIndex::range(role,start,end,sep) landed in bd-43ac5e (closed) with tests — but it is DEAD CODE: nothing calls it.

Repro: `nlir -e '0^2' --context-file <ctx with assistant msgs> --mode det` → `parse error at token 1: unexpected token Message(Assistant) after statement`. The lexer emits `0` (Number) then `^2` (Message{Assistant,2}); the parser has no infix `^` handling so `M^N` never forms.

parser.rs:17-19 explicitly says the `M^N` message-range infix form is DEFERRED. So three pieces are missing:
1. AST: no `Expr::MessageRange { role, start, end }` node (only `Expr::Message{role,index}`).
2. Parser: no infix `^` production — must distinguish prefix `^N` (message index) from infix `M^N` (range) per the lexer.rs:801 note "parser distinguishes prefix vs infix `^`".
3. Eval: no arm to call MessageIndex::range (needs adding to both eval() and eval_parallel_safe(); it is read-only → parallel-safe).

Lanes: AST+parser = msm-0; eval+messages range logic = aur-1 (I wrote the range fn). Proposing aur-1 takes the whole vertical (small, and I own the range logic + eval) pending msm-0 OK on touching parser.rs, to avoid a multi-round handoff. Filed by aur-1.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: parallelism CLI flag resolved then dropped: override never reaches the scheduler
description:
Follow-up to bd-149949 (msm-0's note: "--parallelism 1 did not force sequential in the sleep test — flag->scheduler wiring gap"). Confirmed + fixed.

Root cause: run_eval / run_repl call resolve_defaults(&cfg, &cli_overrides(cli)) which DOES resolve the CLI parallelism override into settings.parallelism, but only settings.mode is passed to evaluate(). The Evaluator reads its parallelism from config.defaults.parallelism (the frozen evaluate(expr,&Config,&mut Context,Mode) signature has no parallelism param), so settings.parallelism is computed then silently dropped -- the CLI flag had no effect.

Fix (main.rs resolve_config, the single choke point all eval paths share): after loading, apply cfg.defaults.parallelism = cli.parallelism.max(1) when set, so the override reaches the scheduler via the config the Evaluator already reads. Respects the frozen evaluate() signature (no API change).

Verified with a sleep-2s command operator, ~a&~b&~c: default(8)=2s (concurrent), parallelism 1=6s (sequential), parallelism 2=4s (chunked 2+1). The parallelism=1->sequential eval behaviour is already locked by the det_seq cache test. Found+fixed by aur-1 while driving nlir in llm mode.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Lexer: `_` echo operator can't tokenise (lex_bare consumes `_` mid-token)
description:
The SPEC `_` echo operator (op: "_", the det-echo test `xxx_2` → "xxx xxx") cannot be tokenised. lex_bare (src/lexer.rs ~line 369) treats `_` as a bare-token continuation char (`c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '_'`) — a consequence of allowing `_`-prefixed system keys like `_sep`. So `xxx_2` lexes as a single Bare("xxx_2") and the configured `_` operator never splits it; the evaluator returns the input unchanged.

Repro: with a config operator `echo: { op: "_", arity: 2, fixity: infix, command: ... }`, `nlir -e 'xxx_2'` (det) yields "xxx_2" instead of "xxx xxx".

This blocks SPEC det-echo. The fix is in the lexer lane (msm-0): disambiguate the `_` echo operator from `_`-in-bare-token / `_`-prefixed keys — e.g. only treat leading `_` as a bare/key start (not a mid-token continuation), or let a configured operator sigil `_` win when it appears between operands, while `_name` at a key position still lexes as a key. Command realisation itself (bd-3c1e6d) is verified working via a non-`_` sigil in src/eval.rs tests; only the `_`-sigil lexing is blocked. Filed by aur-1 from the eval SPEC-completion batch (bd-c85dee/bd-02a795/bd-3c1e6d).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Parser: stack overflow on deeply-nested input (fuzz-found) — add recursion depth guard
description:
A fuzz test (no-panic corpus over tokenize+parse) found that the recursive-descent Pratt parser has NO recursion-depth bound, so deeply-nested input (e.g. `nlir -e '(((((…a…)))))'` ~256+ deep, or nested `[a,[a,…`) recurses until the stack overflows → SIGABRT (exit 134), crashing the CLI instead of returning a clean error. DoS-class robustness bug.

Fix: added `MAX_PARSE_DEPTH = 256` and a depth guard on the `expr` recursion chokepoint (Parser gains a `depth` field; `expr` increments/checks/decrements around the renamed `expr_inner`). All recursion (groups `(`, lists `[`, prefix ops, backtick, message index) routes through the guarded `expr`, so deeper nesting now returns `parse error: expression nesting too deep` instead of overflowing. Verified: depth 100 → ok, depth 256/300/2000 → clean error (exit 1). 256 is safely below both the ~2MB test-thread and 8MB main-thread overflow points. Added `fuzz_tokenize_and_parse_never_panic` (deterministic xorshift corpus, no proptest dep) to lock the no-panic guarantee. Filed + fixed by msm-0.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Bare float literals do not lex: decimal point rejected (3.14, 0.5, 2**0.5)
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. Bare decimal/float number literals fail to lex:
- `nlir -e '3.14'` => lex error at position 1: unexpected character '.'
- `nlir -e '0.5'` => same
- `nlir -e '2**0.5'` => lex error on the '.'
- `nlir -e '1.5+2.5'` => same

But float RESULTS work fine (`7/2` => 3.5) and QUOTED decimals coerce (`'3.14'+'2.86'` => 6). So you can COMPUTE floats but cannot WRITE them as literals — an asymmetry confirming this is a gap, not intended integer-only.

Root: lexer.rs emits numbers as `Token::Bare(digit-run)` (as_number accepts `s.bytes().all(is_ascii_digit)`), and the standalone number scanner does not consume a `.`+fractional part, so `.` hits the "unexpected character / not a configured sigil" path. Fix: extend the bare-number lexing to accept an optional single `.` + fractional digits (and reject a bare trailing/double dot), producing a Token::Number(f64) or a float-parseable Bare. Watch the interaction with `^N.M`? (no — ranges are M^N, not dotted) and with `_sep`/list commas. Lexer lane = msm-0. Filed by aur-1.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1
